title: Distributed Firewall Rule Hit Counts by Application Tier
spl:
index=vmware sourcetype="vmware:nsx:dfw" earliest=-7d
| lookup nsx_vm_tier.csv vm_name AS src OUTPUT tier AS src_tier
| stats count by src_tier, rule_id, rule_name, action
| eventstats sum(count) as tier_total by src_tier, action
| eval pct=round(100*count/tier_total,2)
| sort src_tier, -count
| table src_tier, rule_id, rule_name, action, count, pct
description: Grouping DFW hits by application tier proves which segmentation boundaries are exercised in production and identifies stale allow rules that see no traffic. This reduces attack surface during audits and prevents accidental removal of rules that still protect critical tiers.
value: Grouping DFW hits by application tier proves which segmentation boundaries are exercised in production and identifies stale allow rules that see no traffic. This reduces attack surface during audits and prevents accidental removal of rules that still protect critical tiers.
implementation: (1) Maintain `nsx_vm_tier.csv` mapping VM names to tier labels. (2) Refresh weekly from CMDB. (3) Alert when production tier shows zero hits on mandatory allow rules for seven days.
visualization: Heatmap (tier Ă— rule hits), Bar chart (hits by tier), Table (zero-hit rules).

references.title: Splunk Lantern â€” use case library

detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
â€˘ Install and configure the required add-on or app: `vmware_nsx_addon`.
â€˘ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=vmware` `sourcetype="vmware:nsx:dfw"` with fields `rule_id`, `rule_name`, `action`, `src`, `tier`.
â€˘ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 â€” Configure data collection
(1) Maintain `nsx_vm_tier.csv` mapping VM names to tier labels. (2) Refresh weekly from CMDB. (3) Alert when production tier shows zero hits on mandatory allow rules for seven days.

Step 2 â€” Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=vmware sourcetype="vmware:nsx:dfw" earliest=-7d
| lookup nsx_vm_tier.csv vm_name AS src OUTPUT tier AS src_tier
| stats count by src_tier, rule_id, rule_name, action
| eventstats sum(count) as tier_total by src_tier, action
| eval pct=round(100*count/tier_total,2)
| sort src_tier, -count
| table src_tier, rule_id, rule_name, action, count, pct
```

Understanding this SPL

**Distributed Firewall Rule Hit Counts by Application Tier** â€” Grouping DFW hits by application tier proves which segmentation boundaries are exercised in production and identifies stale allow rules that see no traffic. This reduces attack surface during audits and prevents accidental removal of rules that still protect critical tiers.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=vmware` `sourcetype="vmware:nsx:dfw"` with fields `rule_id`, `rule_name`, `action`, `src`, `tier`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `vmware_nsx_addon`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedâ€”rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: vmware; **sourcetype**: vmware:nsx:dfw. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

â€˘ Scopes the data: index=vmware, sourcetype="vmware:nsx:dfw", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
â€˘ Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
â€˘ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by src_tier, rule_id, rule_name, action** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
â€˘ `eventstats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by src_tier, action** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
â€˘ `eval` defines or adjusts **pct** â€” often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
â€˘ Orders rows with `sort` â€” combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
â€˘ Pipeline stage (see **Distributed Firewall Rule Hit Counts by Application Tier**): table src_tier, rule_id, rule_name, action, count, pct

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 â€” Validate
Compare with VMware NSX Manager (monitoring, alarms, and relevant inventory) for the same time window.

Step 4 â€” Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Heatmap (tier Ă— rule hits), Bar chart (hits by tier), Table (zero-hit rules).
grandmaExplanation: Grouping DFW hits by application tier proves which segmentation boundaries are exercised in production and identifies stale allow rules that see no traffic â€” so traffic problems, routing mistakes, and risky changes surface fast.
knownFalsePositives: Faults during planned maintenance, hardware refresh, or after firmware upgrades.
